docs.text: Architecture Guide This section is the main technical map of LiRAYS-SCADA for future maintainers. It explains how the backend, frontend, protocol, persistence, authentication, SDKs, and packaging fit together so you can make safe changes without reverse-engineering the repo every time. How To Use This Guide Start with System Overview to understand boundaries and runtime topology. Read Backend Runtime Architecture and Realtime Protocol and Engine before changing server behavior. Read Frontend Architecture before touching Svelte route/layout logic, tree behavior, or graph runtime. Read Data and Persistence Architecture and Authentication and Security Architecture before any schema, auth, or storage changes. Use Clients and Operational CLI and Build, Packaging, and Release Architecture when changes affect SDKs, tooling, or distribution. docs.text: Authentication and Security Architecture Security Modes LiRAYS-SCADA supports two broad modes: Auth disabled ( auth.enabled=false ): no route protection, backend treats callers as admin-equivalent for WS role defaults. Auth enabled ( auth.enabled=true ): route/middleware enforcement + session and PAT validation. Auth-enabled mode requires auth.secret ( AUTH_SECRET ) to be configured. Identity Types User sessions (cookie-based, browser-first) Personal Access Tokens (PAT, bearer header, automation/client-first) Role model is minimal: admin operator Session Flow First-time setup creates admin user via /api/auth/setup . /api/auth/login validates password hash (Argon2) and creates signed access+refresh JWT pair stored in session DB. Browser stores lirays_session cookie (HTTP-only, SameSite=Lax, Secure when TLS enabled). Middleware reads cookie, validates access token, and transparently rotates session via refresh token when needed. Sessions are persisted in sled and survive process restart. PAT Flow PATs are generated and revoked via lirays CLI, not via HTTP endpoints. Token format: pat_<id>.<secret> Server stores only Argon2 hash of secret and expiry metadata. Middleware accepts PAT via Authorization: Bearer <token> and resolves role. PATs are ideal for SDK/integration clients that should not use browser cookies. Route Protection authenticated_only middleware: requires valid PAT or valid/refreshable session admin_only middleware: same as above plus role must be admin Protected surfaces include: /ws view APIs ( /api/views* , with write actions admin-only) Auth endpoints remain publicly callable but enforce setup/login invariants. Realtime Authorization Even after middleware, command-level role checks are enforced in realtime engine: namespace mutation commands are admin-only read/write/subscription commands allowed for operator and admin This gives defense in depth between route-level access and operation-level authorization. TLS Behavior When tls.enabled=true : server serves HTTPS/WSS via rustls certificate/key loaded from configured paths, or auto-generated self-signed cert when tls.auto=true session cookie is marked Secure Auto-generated certs are suitable for local/internal environments, not public PKI trust chains. Hardening Notes Keep auth.secret stable and high-entropy. Prefer TLS-enabled deployments for session-cookie confidentiality. Rotate PATs and keep expiry bounded. Restrict filesystem access to data_dir and settings files. Review packaging service units for least privilege assumptions in your target OS.

docs.text: Backend Runtime Architecture Entrypoints Runtime binary: src/main.rs ( lirays-scada ) CLI binary: src/bin/liraysctl.rs ( lirays ) The server path is: main -> app::run -> http::run_http_server Runtime Composition ( AppState ) AppState is the shared dependency container injected into Axum handlers and middleware: views : ViewManager (SQLite) users : UserManager (SQLite + Argon2) patoken_manager : PATokenManager (SQLite + Argon2) auth : AuthConfig (enabled flag, secret, TTLs, secure cookie toggle) var_manager : VariableManager (sled realtime namespace/value engine) session_manager : SessionManager (sled + JWT-based session lifecycle) Route Mounting Strategy Server routes are mounted in order: /ws (auth-gated when enabled) /api/views* (mix of authenticated/admin-only) /api/auth/* /api-docs/openapi.json and /swagger static fallback for all unmatched routes The final fallback serves embedded frontend assets; this makes backend the single public entrypoint. Resource Modules views Owns view CRUD, pagination, sorting, search, and entry-point selection. Maintains invariant that exactly one entry-point view exists. Initializes DB with a default Root View if table is empty. Returns envelope responses and OpenAPI annotations. auth /api/auth/setup : bootstrap first admin when auth is enabled and admin does not exist. /api/auth/login : username/password login -> session cookie. /api/auth/status : route guards use this to drive redirects. /api/auth/logout : revokes session. docs Serves OpenAPI JSON generated via utoipa . Serves Swagger UI HTML (CDN-hosted swagger assets). static_files Serves compile-time embedded files from frontend/build ( include_dir ). Includes SPA fallback behavior ( index.html ) for deep links. user and patoken Provide schema and manager logic. No public PAT CRUD HTTP endpoints yet; PAT lifecycle is handled via lirays CLI. Middleware Design Two middleware layers enforce auth at route boundaries: authenticated_only : any authenticated role (admin/operator) admin_only : admin role only Role extraction order: Authorization: Bearer <pat> (PAT verification) lirays_session cookie (access token) Session refresh flow using refresh token if access token expired If auth is disabled, middleware passes through. Background Processes Launched during startup: Variable dirty flush loop ( flush_ms , min 5s) Session purge loop (every 12 hours) Metrics writer loop (every 5 seconds, when enabled) These loops are intentionally detached from request handlers. Shutdown Behavior Graceful shutdown listens for SIGTERM/SIGINT (or Ctrl+C fallback), flushes dirty realtime values, and then exits. This avoids recent value loss in the write-back cache model. Adding A New HTTP Domain Create src/http/resources/<domain>/ with mod.rs , service, model, schema init. Add ensure_schema call in http::ensure_schema if persistent. Mount routes in run_http_server with proper middleware. Extend OpenAPI docs if externally visible. Update frontend/API client layers if user-facing.

docs.text: Build, Packaging, and Release Architecture Build Dependency Chain Production backend binary embeds frontend static assets at compile time. Required order: Generate frontend TS protos ( frontend npm run generate:proto ). Build frontend ( frontend/build ). Build Rust backend ( cargo build --release ). src/http/resources/static_files/service.rs uses include_dir! on frontend/build , so missing frontend build artifacts will break or stale the embedded UI. Tooling Surface Primary orchestrator: Makefile Notable targets: app build/test: frontend , test packaging: deb , rpm , mac SDK docs/publish: rust-client-* , python-client-* Docker Build Dockerfile uses 3 stages: frontend builder (Node 24 + protobuf) backend builder (Rust + embedded frontend build output) alpine runtime image ( lirays-scada + lirays ) This produces a single-container runtime with embedded web UI. OS Packaging Architecture Debian ( packaging/debian ) Installs binaries + systemd service + default settings. Post-install creates service user/group, data dir, and seeds settings.yaml from default if missing. Service runs with dedicated user and strict filesystem write path. RPM ( packaging/rpm ) Similar service model to Debian. %post handles service enable/start and first-time settings seeding. Includes dedicated lirays service account setup in %pre . macOS ( packaging/macos ) Builds universal binary package ( pkgbuild / productbuild ). Installs LaunchDaemon plist + binaries + settings defaults. Postinstall script bootstraps and kickstarts daemon. Runtime Service Model Packaged service managers call: server binary with explicit config path persistent data directory outside binary location restart-on-failure behavior This keeps upgrades mostly config/data preserving. Release Artifacts Output directory: distributions/ Contains platform artifacts ( .deb , .rpm , .pkg ) produced by make targets. Safe Release Checklist Rebuild frontend and backend from clean state. Run frontend unit tests and Rust tests. Verify protocol compatibility for frontend + SDKs. Validate packaging scripts against current config defaults. Smoke-test auth-enabled and auth-disabled startup modes. Verify service restart behavior on each target platform.
docs.title: Build and Release

docs.text: Clients and Operational CLI lirays Operational CLI ( src/liraysctl/* ) The CLI is an operator/admin control plane for local host maintenance. Command families: user lifecycle: create/remove/update admin/operator PAT lifecycle: generate/list/revoke settings inspection/update service restart helpers (systemd/launchd) metrics watch helper Key architecture traits: Reads runtime settings using same settings resolver model. Opens static.db directly for user/PAT operations. Uses interactive prompts for secrets and token metadata. Encourages explicit operational workflows (for example, bootstrap URL after admin removal). Rust SDK ( clients/rust-client ) Design: Async command client over websocket + protobuf. Correlates command responses using per-command ids and pending oneshot map. Applies command timeout per request. Exposes typed helper APIs for create/list/set/get/edit/bulk operations. Subscription model: Opens a separate websocket for SUB streams. Returns async stream of (var_id, typed_value) tuples. Connection modes: anonymous ( connect ) PAT-authenticated ( connect_with_pat ) explicit options ( connect_with_options ) Python SDK ( clients/python-client ) Design: PyO3 extension module wrapping local Rust SDK crate. Blocking Python API, async work executed on internal Tokio runtime. Mirrors Rust SDK surface with Pythonic classes and typed stubs. This avoids duplicating protocol logic and keeps Rust SDK as protocol implementation source. When You Add A New Protocol Capability Update sequence should be treated as mandatory checklist: proto/namespace/*.proto server implementation ( src/rtdata/variable.rs ) frontend command builder and WS client Rust SDK command/protocol wrappers Python bindings surface if externally needed docs and demos Skipping any of these usually creates integration drift. Integrator Guidance Prefer PAT auth for service-to-service access. Treat timeout handling as part of normal operation (network/restart aware). Use list/get before set in automation flows when type/constraint certainty matters. Keep SDK and server versions aligned with shared proto schema expectations.
docs.title: Clients and CLI

docs.text: Data and Persistence Architecture Data Root Layout Runtime data lives under configured data_dir (default ./data ): rt_data/ : realtime namespace + value storage (sled) sessions/ : session store (sled) static.db : SQLite relational store metrics/ : optional realtime + historical metrics files certificates/ : auto-generated self-signed cert/key when TLS auto mode is enabled Realtime Store ( rt_data ) Implemented by VariableManager using two sled trees: mainTree ( items_tree ): namespace metadata valuesTree ( values_tree ): persisted variable values Key conventions: Namespace node metadata key: parent_path/\0name Variable value key: normalized variable path ( /Area/Tag ) In-memory values_cache is preloaded at startup from metadata + persisted values. Session Store ( sessions ) SessionManager uses a sled tree with two key families: session payload: user_session:{session_id} user index: user_session_access:{username}:{session_id} This supports: quick session lookup full user session revocation periodic purge of expired refresh tokens Relational Store ( static.db ) SQLite tables initialized on startup: users : username, password hash, role patokens : token metadata + hash + expiry views : canvas documents + entry-point marker No external migration framework is currently used; table creation is managed in code with CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS . Write Durability Model Realtime Set operations are write-back: Accepted values update in-memory cache immediately. Dirty map records latest value per variable. Flush loop persists coalesced values to sled on interval. Graceful shutdown performs explicit final flush. This improves throughput while preserving eventual durability. Metrics Files When enabled: metrics_rt.txt : rolling human-readable snapshot (rewritten every 5s) metrics_hist.csv : append-only historical metrics timeline Metrics include command counts, average latencies, and event fan-out counters. Configuration Resolution Effective config precedence: Environment variable YAML setting file Built-in default Settings loader resolves each setting using SettingSpec and tracks source ( env , file , default ). Operational Implications Back up both static.db and sled dirs for full recoverability. Treat settings.yaml as persistent operator-managed config; packaging seeds defaults but does not overwrite existing user config. For auth-enabled deployments, persist and protect auth.secret consistently across restarts.
docs.title: Data and Persistence

docs.text: Frontend Architecture Runtime Model Frontend is a SvelteKit SPA ( ssr = false , prerender = true ) built to frontend/build and embedded by backend. In local dev, Vite proxies: /api -> backend HTTP /ws -> backend websocket Route Groups Root +layout.svelte : global theme/snackbar/navigation-progress shell. (guest) routes: /auth/login , /auth/setup . (app) routes: designer workspace (views list + editor). runtime/* routes: runtime-only display mode for operators and production playback. Guards call /api/auth/status and redirect according to: auth enabled/disabled admin bootstrap state authenticated vs anonymous operator vs admin role Workspace Shell ( (app)/+layout.svelte ) This is the orchestration hub for designer mode: Left pane: VariableTree Right pane: views list or view editor Graph canvas: @xyflow/svelte Inspector pane: binding and node metadata editing Dialogs: view removal, tree deletion, namespace builder, leave-view guard Primary UI states: rightPaneMode : views-list or view-editor (route-derived) canvasMode : edit or play multi-selection mode for bulk tree deletion Data Channels REST Used mainly for view management and auth status/logout: /api/views* /api/auth/status /api/auth/logout All API calls go through apiFetch , which centralizes API-path 401 handling. WebSocket TagStreamClient is the single transport for: Tree namespace operations ( LIST , ADD , DEL , EDIT_META , ADD_BULK ) Realtime value subscription ( SUB/UNSUB + push events) Value writes ( SET ) createPageTagRealtimeProvider wraps this client with page-level active/id-tracking semantics. Tree Architecture Core tree modules: tree-store.ts : normalized state (nodes map + root ids + expanded/loading/errored sets) server-adapter.ts : maps backend ListResponse into frontend TreeNode tree-remote-reconcile.ts : applies pushed TreeChanged events incrementally or by reload request VariableTree.svelte : virtualization, keyboard navigation, dialogs, selection UX Tree updates are eventually consistent with realtime events, without full refresh unless requested. Graph + Widget Architecture Graph nodes represent widget assets with tag bindings. assets/registry.ts : runtime asset registry PlantAssetNode.svelte : dispatches to Svelte or custom-element runtime CustomElementAssetHost.svelte : bridges Svelte node data to web components scada/plugins/internal-widgets/* : built-in widget plugin declarations and custom-element runtime shell Live values flow through live-utils.ts , which computes tracked tag ids and patches only impacted nodes when possible. Designer vs Runtime Separation Designer ( /views/:id ): edit graph, assign bindings, manage namespace. Runtime ( /runtime/:id ): read-only graph layout + realtime values + allowed writes per widget binding permissions. Both share rendering primitives; runtime strips editing affordances and always enables realtime provider. Frontend Extension Points New widget type: add declaration under internal-widgets/widgets , ensure manifest bindings and runtime registration. New backend command usage: add builder in core/ws/command-ws-client.ts and handling in TagStreamClient . New page-level mode: add route group and guard updates in layout loads.

docs.text: Realtime Protocol and Engine Contract Source Of Truth The canonical wire contract is in proto/namespace/*.proto : commands.proto : command and response envelopes types.proto : namespace/value/domain payloads events.proto : pushed events enums.proto : enum values for status/types/events Rust backend and Rust SDK compile these protos via prost-build ; frontend compiles TypeScript bindings via ts-proto . WebSocket Semantics Endpoint: /ws Command input formats: protobuf binary frames JSON text frames Response output mirrors input format style (binary for protobuf command, JSON text for JSON command). Event pushes are emitted continuously for subscribed sessions. Command Surface Supported commands in VariableManager::exec_cmd : Namespace mutation: Add , Del , AddBulk , EditMeta Namespace/value query: List , Get Value write: Set Streaming control: Sub , Unsub Response envelope always includes: typed response payload (matching command) OperationStatus optional error_msg Authorization Matrix Admin-only commands: Add , Del , AddBulk , EditMeta Admin and Operator: List , Get , Set , Sub , Unsub Unauthorized calls return error responses; frontend and SDKs surface these as operation failures. Internal Storage Model VariableManager keeps three core structures: items_tree (sled): namespace metadata keys parent/\0name values_tree (sled): persisted variable values keyed by full variable path values_cache (in-memory): hot working set with dtype and constraints Write path is write-back: Set mutates values_cache value is marked dirty in dirty_values periodic loop batches dirty entries to values_tree Event Fan-Out Subscribers register per-session bounded channels. Broadcast is non-blocking. Slow/full listeners drop batches instead of backpressuring producers. Closed listeners are evicted. Event filtering is per connection: VarValueEv sent only for subscribed ids TreeChangedEv sent only when tree-change subscription is active Tree Change Model Namespace mutations emit TreeChanged events with FolderChanged entries. Incremental mode: removed ids + new folders + new variables Reload mode: reload=true tells clients to refresh parent folder content Frontend tree reconciliation logic is built around this dual mode. Constraint Validation Before accepting new values: Numeric: dtype checks + min/max bounds Text: options whitelist + max length Boolean: bool/int/float coercion rules Validation is centralized in validate_constraints . Cross-Layer Protocol Gotcha Response and Event protobuf envelopes share overlapping wire tags. Frontend TagStreamClient handles this by decoding as Response first and falling back to Event when no pending command matches cmd_id . If you evolve proto envelope fields, verify this logic still holds. Adding A New Realtime Command Add command/response in proto. Rebuild generated code in backend/frontend/SDKs. Implement server command branch in VariableManager::exec_cmd . Add frontend command builder and client handling. Add SDK support in Rust client and Python binding if needed. Add tests for auth, status mapping, and timeout/error paths.

docs.text: System Overview Repository Shape LiRAYS-SCADA is a monorepo with one primary runtime and several supporting products: src/ : Rust backend runtime ( lirays-scada ) and operational CLI ( lirays ). frontend/ : SvelteKit SPA, compiled to static assets and embedded into backend binary. proto/ : Shared protocol contract (commands, responses, events, data types). clients/rust-client/ : Rust SDK for external integrators. clients/python-client/ : PyO3 wrapper around Rust SDK. packaging/ : deb/rpm/macos service packaging assets. doc/ : user/developer/integrator documentation. Runtime Topology A single server process ( lirays-scada ) hosts: REST endpoints (auth + views + docs) WebSocket command/event endpoint ( /ws ) Static frontend fallback (embedded frontend/build ) State is split across: sled for realtime namespace and values sled for session persistence SQLite for users, PAT metadata, and view definitions High-Level Request Paths REST path Browser calls /api/* . Axum route + middleware checks auth/role when enabled. Resource manager ( views , users , etc.) reads/writes SQLite or session store. Standard response envelope is returned for API endpoints. Realtime path Client opens /ws and sends protobuf (binary) or JSON text commands. VariableManager::exec_cmd executes command with role checks. Command response is returned (status + optional error message). Asynchronous Event pushes (value updates / tree changes) are broadcast to subscribed sessions. Startup And Lifecycle src/main.rs calls app::run() . Config is resolved from --config , YAML, env overrides, and defaults. Data directories are created. DB/schema initialization runs for SQLite tables. Managers are constructed and shared through AppState . Background loops start: dirty-value flush loop session purge loop optional metrics writer loop HTTP/HTTPS listener starts with graceful shutdown; on shutdown dirty cache is flushed. Core Architectural Decisions Single-process deployment: simpler ops on low-resource hosts. Unified /ws command and event channel: low overhead and consistent protocol. Hybrid persistence model: sqlite for relational entities, sled for high-frequency realtime state. Optional auth and TLS: same binary can run dev-friendly or production-hardened. Frontend embedded in server: no separate static hosting required in production. Main Extension Seams Add backend domains: new src/http/resources/<domain> module + mount in src/http/mod.rs . Add new realtime commands: update proto , server VariableManager::exec_cmd , frontend WS command builder, SDK clients. Add new widget types: register internal plugin declaration in frontend widget registry. Add packaging/runtime profiles: update settings.default.yaml + service units/scripts under packaging/ .
docs.title: System Overview

docs.text: Report Bug To report a bug, open an issue on GitHub: LiRAYS-SCADA Issues Before You Open A Bug Report Search existing issues first to avoid duplicates. Confirm the behavior is reproducible. Gather logs, screenshots, and environment details. What To Include Use this structure in your issue: Summary : short title with the problem and area affected. Environment : OS and version LiRAYS-SCADA version Deployment mode (Linux package, macOS package, Docker, dev run) Steps to reproduce : exact sequence from a clean starting point. Expected behavior : what should happen. Actual behavior : what happened instead. Evidence : logs error messages screenshots/video Impact : how this affects operations (severity, frequency, workaround). Tips Include a minimal reproducible scenario. If the issue is intermittent, include approximate occurrence rate. Do not share secrets or credentials in public issues.

docs.text: Development Environment This guide explains how to prepare a local workstation for LiRAYS-SCADA development. Prerequisites Rust toolchain with Edition 2024 support Node.js 24+ Protocol Buffers compiler ( protoc ) Local Run Workflow 1. Build Frontend Assets From the repository root: cd frontend npm install npm run generate:proto npm run build cd .. 2. Run the Backend Use a config file (for example settings-dev.yaml ) and start the server: cargo run --bin lirays-scada -- --config ./settings-dev.yaml Default local endpoint: http{s}://127.0.0.1:8245 3. Optional Frontend Dev Mode If you are working on frontend code, run the backend first and then start Vite in a second terminal: cd frontend npm run dev Frontend dev URL: http://127.0.0.1:5173

docs.text: Product Vision LiRAYS-SCADA exists to make industrial data and control systems fast, efficient, and accessible. Our vision is to build a high-performance real-time data platform with a minimal resource footprint, capable of running reliably from constrained edge devices to large-scale production environments. On top of this core, LiRAYS-SCADA provides a seamless experience for visualizing, monitoring, and controlling processes with clarity and confidence. We aim to unify three qualities often treated separately: performance, operational usability, and extensibility. LiRAYS-SCADA should feel lightweight in infrastructure cost, powerful in real-time behavior, and practical for day-to-day operations.

docs.text: Configuration This guide explains how to configure LiRAYS-SCADA using settings.yaml , environment variables, and service restart workflows. Configuration File Location Packaged installations use a platform-specific settings.yaml file: Linux (DEB/RPM): /etc/lirays-scada/settings.yaml macOS package: /Library/Application Support/LiRAYSScada/settings.yaml On first install, the package seeds settings.yaml from settings.default.yaml only if settings.yaml does not exist. Package upgrades do not overwrite settings.yaml . Default template files shipped by packages: Linux (DEB/RPM): /etc/lirays-scada/settings.default.yaml macOS package: /Library/Application Support/LiRAYSScada/settings.default.yaml For Docker deployments, you can either: Pass environment variables directly to the container. Mount a settings file and run the server with --config /path/to/settings.yaml . How Configuration Values Are Resolved LiRAYS-SCADA resolves each setting in this order: Environment variable override. Value from settings.yaml . Built-in fallback default. Environment Variable Notes Boolean environment variables accept: true/false , 1/0 , yes/no , on/off . If an environment variable value cannot be parsed, startup fails with a configuration error. If a value exists in settings.yaml but cannot be parsed to the expected type, startup fails with a configuration error. Modify Configuration You can modify configuration in two ways. Option 1: Edit settings.yaml directly Linux: sudo nano /etc/lirays-scada/settings.yaml macOS: sudo nano "/Library/Application Support/LiRAYSScada/settings.yaml" Option 2: Use the lirays helper The CLI can update one parameter at a time: sudo lirays settings-update server.bind_host 0.0.0.0 sudo lirays settings-update auth.enabled true sudo lirays settings-update auth.secret "replace_with_long_random_secret" Inspect effective values (and whether each value came from env/file/default): sudo lirays settings Restart Service After Changes Configuration changes become effective after service restart. Linux (systemd): sudo systemctl restart lirays-scada sudo systemctl status lirays-scada macOS (launchd): sudo launchctl kickstart -k system/com.lirays.liraysscada launchctl print system/com.lirays.liraysscada Cross-platform helper (Linux and macOS): sudo lirays restart-service Docker: # If using docker run docker restart lirays-scada # If using compose docker compose up -d Full Parameter Reference server bind_host Type: string Default in packaged templates: 127.0.0.1 Env var: BIND_HOST Description: Host/interface used by HTTP and WebSocket. Common values: 127.0.0.1 : local-only access. 0.0.0.0 : bind all interfaces. bind_port Type: integer ( u16 ) Default: 8245 Env var: BIND_PORT Description: Port used by HTTP and WebSocket. paths data_dir Type: path or null Env var: DATA_DIR Default in packaged templates: Linux: /var/lib/lirays-scada/data macOS: /Library/Application Support/LiRAYSScada/data If unset/missing in non-packaged runs, fallback is {current_working_directory}/data . Description: Root directory for runtime data, including DB files, sessions, metrics, and TLS auto-generated certificates. tls enabled Type: boolean Default: false Env var: TLS_ENABLE Description: Enables TLS for both HTTP and WebSocket endpoints. auto Type: boolean Default: true Env var: TLS_AUTO Description: If tls.enabled=true and cert/key are not provided, auto-generates a self-signed certificate pair under ${data_dir}/certificates . cert_path Type: path or null Default: null Env var: TLS_CERT_PATH Description: PEM certificate path when using manual TLS certificate configuration. key_path Type: path or null Default: null Env var: TLS_KEY_PATH Description: PEM private key path when using manual TLS certificate configuration. TLS validation rules: If tls.enabled=false , TLS cert settings are ignored. If tls.enabled=true and cert_path + key_path are both set, those files are used. If tls.enabled=true , both cert/key are missing, and tls.auto=true , a self-signed pair is generated. If tls.enabled=true and only one of cert/key is set, startup fails. If tls.enabled=true , both cert/key are missing, and tls.auto=false , startup fails. metrics real_time Type: boolean Default: false Env var: METRICS_REAL_TIME Description: Writes rolling snapshot metrics to ${data_dir}/metrics/metrics_rt.txt . historic Type: boolean Default: false Env var: METRICS_HISTORIC Description: Appends historical metrics to ${data_dir}/metrics/metrics_hist.csv . persistence flush_ms Type: integer ( u64 , milliseconds) Default: 15000 Env var: PERSIST_FLUSH_MS Description: Flush interval for dirty values. Runtime rule: minimum effective value is 5000 ; lower values are automatically clamped to 5000 . logger level Type: string enum Default: info Env var: LOGGER_LEVEL Accepted values: off , error , warn (or warning ), info , debug , trace . Description: Global application log verbosity. auth enabled Type: boolean Default: false Env var: AUTH_ENABLED Description: Enables session-protected UI/API. secret Type: string or null Default: null Env var: AUTH_SECRET Description: HMAC secret for session signing. Required when: auth.enabled=true . access_ttl Type: integer ( u64 , seconds) Default: 3600 Env var: ACCESS_TTL Description: Access token/session TTL. refresh_ttl Type: integer ( u64 , seconds) Default: 86400 Env var: REFRESH_TTL Description: Refresh token/session TTL. Authentication validation rules: If auth.enabled=true and auth.secret is missing (and AUTH_SECRET is not set), startup fails. Example Baseline Configuration Use the path that matches your platform for paths.data_dir : Linux: /var/lib/lirays-scada/data macOS: /Library/Application Support/LiRAYSScada/data server: bind_host: 127.0.0.1 bind_port: 8245 paths: data_dir: /var/lib/lirays-scada/data tls: enabled: false auto: true cert_path: null key_path: null metrics: real_time: false historic: false persistence: flush_ms: 15000 logger: level: info auth: enabled: false secret: null access_ttl: 3600 refresh_ttl: 86400 Production-Recommended Configuration For production, use explicit authentication, explicit TLS certificates, and conservative logging. server: bind_host: 0.0.0.0 bind_port: 8245 paths: data_dir: /var/lib/lirays-scada/data tls: enabled: true auto: false cert_path: /etc/lirays-scada/tls/server.crt key_path: /etc/lirays-scada/tls/server.key metrics: real_time: true historic: true persistence: flush_ms: 10000 logger: level: info auth: enabled: true secret: "replace_with_long_random_secret" access_ttl: 3600 refresh_ttl: 86400 Production hardening guidance: Use a long, random auth.secret (at least 32 bytes of entropy). Restrict permissions on settings.yaml , TLS key files, and the data directory. Prefer fixed certificate files from your PKI or ACME workflow ( tls.auto=false ). Keep logger.level=info for normal operations; switch to debug only for incident diagnostics. Backup ${data_dir} regularly. Optional secret generation example: openssl rand -base64 48 Validate Configuration Health After any configuration update: Restart the service. Check service status and logs. Open the UI endpoint ( http://127.0.0.1:8245 or https://... if TLS enabled). If auth is enabled and no admin exists yet, complete initial setup at /auth/setup .

docs.text: Docker Deployment This guide describes a container-based deployment for LiRAYS-SCADA. Before You Start The image references in this page use placeholders. Replace: registry.example.com/lirays-scada:<tag> with your real registry host and release tag. Pull Image docker pull registry.example.com/lirays-scada:<tag> Quick Start ( docker run ) docker run -d \ --name lirays-scada \ --restart unless-stopped \ -p 8245:8245 \ -v lirays-data:/data \ registry.example.com/lirays-scada:<tag> Access URL: http://127.0.0.1:8245 Compose Deployment ( compose.yaml ) services: lirays-scada: image: registry.example.com/lirays-scada:<tag> container_name: lirays-scada restart: unless-stopped ports: - "8245:8245" volumes: - lirays-data:/data # Optional custom config: # - ./settings.yaml:/app/settings.yaml:ro # command: ["--config", "/app/settings.yaml"] volumes: lirays-data: Start and stop: docker compose up -d docker compose down Custom Configuration Option 1: Mount settings.yaml docker run -d \ --name lirays-scada \ --restart unless-stopped \ -p 8245:8245 \ -v lirays-data:/data \ -v "$(pwd)/settings.yaml:/app/settings.yaml:ro" \ registry.example.com/lirays-scada:<tag> \ --config /app/settings.yaml Option 2: Use Environment Variables docker run -d \ --name lirays-scada \ --restart unless-stopped \ -p 8245:8245 \ -v lirays-data:/data \ -e AUTH_ENABLED=true \ -e AUTH_SECRET="replace_with_strong_secret" \ -e TLS_ENABLE=false \ -e METRICS_REAL_TIME=true \ registry.example.com/lirays-scada:<tag> For complete variable and precedence details, see Settings File Configuration . Verify docker ps --filter name=lirays-scada docker logs --tail=200 -f lirays-scada docker inspect lirays-scada --format '{{.State.Status}}' Upgrade docker run workflow: docker pull registry.example.com/lirays-scada:<new-tag> docker stop lirays-scada docker rm lirays-scada docker run -d \ --name lirays-scada \ --restart unless-stopped \ -p 8245:8245 \ -v lirays-data:/data \ registry.example.com/lirays-scada:<new-tag> Compose workflow: docker compose pull docker compose up -d Remove Deployment Keep volume (preserve data): docker stop lirays-scada docker rm lirays-scada Full cleanup (container, image, and volume): docker stop lirays-scada || true docker rm lirays-scada || true docker rmi registry.example.com/lirays-scada:<tag> || true docker volume rm lirays-data || true

docs.text: Linux Installation This guide covers package-based installation on Linux. Before You Start Download URLs in this page use placeholders. Replace <version> and architecture values with real release artifacts. Available package patterns: lirays-scada_<version>_amd64.deb lirays-scada_<version>_arm64.deb lirays-scada-<version>-1.x86_64.rpm lirays-scada-<version>-1.aarch64.rpm Example links: DEB amd64 DEB arm64 RPM x86_64 RPM aarch64 Download with curl (Optional) curl -L -o lirays-scada_<version>_amd64.deb "https://downloads.example.com/lirays-scada/linux/lirays-scada_<version>_amd64.deb" curl -L -o lirays-scada-<version>-1.x86_64.rpm "https://downloads.example.com/lirays-scada/linux/lirays-scada-<version>-1.x86_64.rpm" Install Debian and Ubuntu sudo apt install ./lirays-scada_<version>_<architecture>.deb RHEL, Rocky, Fedora sudo dnf install ./lirays-scada-<version>-1.<architecture>.rpm The installer registers the lirays-scada.service systemd service. docs.text: First Steps This tutorial walks through a clean first run of LiRAYS-SCADA using only the workflows documented in this repository. Goal By the end of this tutorial, you should have: a running LiRAYS-SCADA service, a reachable web interface, a verified baseline configuration, and a clear next step for your deployment. Step 1: Install LiRAYS-SCADA Choose one installation method: Docker Linux package macOS package Complete the install and confirm the service starts. Step 2: Confirm Service Health Open the local UI endpoint: http://127.0.0.1:8245 If the page does not load, verify service status and logs with the commands in your selected installation guide. Step 3: Confirm Authentication Mode LiRAYS-SCADA can run with authentication enabled or disabled. If authentication is disabled, continue to the next step. If authentication is enabled and this is the first startup, complete the setup flow from the browser ( /api/auth/setup backend flow). To review related settings, use Settings File Configuration . Step 4: Review Runtime Configuration Check at least these parameters before moving to production-like use: server.bind_host server.bind_port paths.data_dir auth.enabled auth.secret (required when auth is enabled) tls.enabled Apply any changes and restart the service as described in Settings File Configuration . Step 5: Validate Operations Baseline Run a minimal validation pass: Open the web UI and confirm it is responsive. Confirm the service process is healthy using your platform service manager. Confirm logs do not show startup or configuration errors. Optional API/docs check: http://127.0.0.1:8245/swagger Next Step After this baseline setup, continue with: Users Guide for operations, Integrators Guide for SDK/client integration, Developers Guide for code-level extension.
